titleStmt.title: Letter from Arthur John Eagleston to the Under-Secretary, Dublin Castle, 4 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Arthur John Eagleston

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from A. J. Eagleston (1870-1944) to Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939), the Under-Secretary, Dublin Castle, confirming that the Petition of Right of John and Agnes Scott, John McCullough and Mary Shearer has been laid before the Lord Lieutenant of Ireland, with a copy of a letter from the War Office.Arthur John Eagleston was a civil servant who served as assistant secretary of the British Home Office from 1913-1932. Sir Matthew Nathan was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
HOME OFFICE, WHITEHALL 4th April, 1916. Sir, I am directed by the Secretary of State to transmit
to you, to be laid before His Excellency the Lord Lieutenant
of Ireland, the accompanying Petition of Right of John and
Agnes Scott, John McCulloch, and Mary Shearer, together with
a copy of a letter from the War Office with reference thereto;
and I am to request that the Petition may be referred to His
Majesty's Attorney-General for Ireland for the favour of his
report whether there is any objection to the document being
endorsed in the usual manner 'Let Right be done'. I am,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant, A.G.Eagleston The Under Secretary, Dublin Castle.
